claims.flip_evidence_text: if, and only if, (A) an Intervening Event has occurred and the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee has determined in good faith, after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel, that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with such directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law; provided that neither the Company Board nor the Special Committee may take any such action ... unless (I) prior to making such Change of Company Recommendation, the Company provides prior written notice to Parent at least four (4) Business Days in advance ... (II) during the Notice Period, the Company shall ... be reasonably available to negotiate with Parent in good faith ... and (III) the Company Board ... has taken into account any amendments ... and has determined in good faith ... that a failure to make such Change of Company Recommendation would still be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law
claims.notes: The claim captures the substantive fiduciary determination but omits material requirements: the timing limit (prior to stockholder approval), mandatory consultation with financial and legal advisors, and the multi-step notice/negotiation/final-determination proviso.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
7.2. Company Acquisition Proposals. <omitted> (b) <omitted> Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth in this Section 7.2(b), the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee may, prior to but not after the time the Requisite Company Stockholder Approvals are obtained, make a Change of Company Recommendation if, and only if, (A) an Intervening Event has occurred and the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee has determined in good faith, after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel, that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with such directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or (B) the Company receives a Company Acquisition Proposal and the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee has determined in good faith, after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel, that such Company Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Company Superior Proposal and that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with such directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law; provided that neither the Company Board nor the Special Committee may take any such action (and the Special Committee may not recommend to the Company Board to take such action) unless (I) prior to making such Change of Company Recommendation, the Company provides prior written notice to Parent at least four (4) Business Days in advance (the “Notice Period”) of its intention to take such action and the basis thereof, which notice shall include, in the case of a Company Superior Proposal, the information required under Section 7.2(e) and, in the case of an Intervening Event, a reasonably detailed description of such Intervening Event, (II) during the Notice Period, the Company shall, and shall cause its employees, financial advisor and outside legal counsel to, be reasonably available to negotiate with Parent in good faith should Parent propose to make amendments or other revisions to the terms and conditions of this Agreement such that, in the case of a Company Superior Proposal, such Company Acquisition Proposal no longer constitutes a Company Superior Proposal or, in the case of an Intervening Event, the failure to take such action would no longer be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law as determined in the good faith judgment of the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee, after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel, and (III) the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee, as the case may be, has taken into account any amendments or other revisions to the terms and conditions of this Agreement agreed to by Parent in writing prior to the end of the Notice Period and has determined in good faith, after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel, that a failure to make such Change of Company Recommendation would still be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law; it being understood that any amendments or other revisions to any Company Acquisition Proposal will be deemed to be a new Company Acquisition Proposal, including for purposes of the Notice Period; provided, however, subsequent to the initial Notice Period, the Notice Period shall be reduced to two (2) Business Days. <omitted> (Pages 43-44)

Question:
COR permitted in response to Intervening Event

Llama answer:
A Change of Company Recommendation (COR) is permitted in response to an Intervening Event if the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee determines in good faith that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with their fiduciary duties under applicable Law.

Canonical claims:
- claim-04bb73677629: A Change of Company Recommendation (COR) is permitted in response to an Intervening Event if the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee determines in good faith that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with their fiduciary duties under applicable Law.

Canonical claims:

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator. I need to evaluate the canonical claim against the contract excerpt, the question, and the model (Llama) answer.

Claim: claim-04bb73677629: "A Change of Company Recommendation (COR) is permitted in response to an Intervening Event if the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee determines in good faith that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with their fiduciary duties under applicable Law."

Let me check the contract excerpt:
- Section 7.2(b) says: "the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee may, prior to but not after the time the Requisite Company Stockholder Approvals are obtained, make a Change of Company Recommendation if, and only if, (A) an Intervening Event has occurred and the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee has determined in good faith, after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel, that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with such directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law..."

The claim states that COR is permitted if the Board/Special Committee determines in good faith that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties.

However, the contract adds additional requirements:
1. Must be prior to but not after the time the Requisite Company Stockholder Approvals are obtained.
2. Must be after consulting with financial advisor and outside legal counsel.
3. Must provide prior written notice at least 4 Business Days in advance (Notice Period).
4. Must be reasonably available to negotiate during Notice Period.
5. Must take into account any amendments by Parent and determine that failure to make COR would still be inconsistent with fiduciary duties.

The claim omits these additional conditions (timing, consultation, notice, negotiation, taking into account amendments). 

Is the claim true, false, or partially true?
The claim says "is permitted ... if ... [condition]". This suggests that the condition stated is sufficient. But the contract says "if, and only if" and then lists (A) with additional sub-conditions (I, II, III). The claim captures the substantive determination requirement but omits the procedural prerequisites (notice, negotiation, consultation requirements) and the timing constraint (prior to stockholder approvals).

However, looking at the claim carefully: "A Change of Company Recommendation (COR) is permitted in response to an Intervening Event if the Company Board ... determines in good faith that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with their fiduciary duties under applicable Law."

This is not fully false because that determination is indeed a necessary condition. But it's not sufficient by itself. The claim presents it as if it's the condition for permission ("if"). In legal contracts, "if" can mean "only if" but usually the claim should capture the full requirements. Since it omits critical prerequisites like the notice period, negotiation, and consultation requirements, and the timing constraint, it's partially true. It captures the core substantive requirement but misses the procedural and timing safeguards.

Wait, let me reconsider. The question is "COR permitted in response to Intervening Event". The Llama answer is essentially identical to the claim. The task is to evaluate the canonical claim, not the Llama answer per se, but using the model answer as context.

Actually, looking at the instructions: "For each claim return: correctness_label, load_bearing_label, flip_evidence_text, notes".

The claim is almost verbatim the Llama answer. The Llama answer is a direct quote or paraphrase of part of the contract. But is it fully correct? The contract says the Board may make a COR "if, and only if, (A) an Intervening Event has occurred and ... has determined in good faith, after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel, that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with such directors' fiduciary duties...".

The claim omits:
- "prior to but not after the time the Requisite Company Stockholder Approvals are obtained"
- "after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel"
- The notice and negotiation requirements (I, II, III)

So the claim is incomplete. Is it "partially_true" or "true"? If a claim states a necessary condition but omits other necessary conditions, it's typically partially_true because it implies sufficiency or completeness that isn't there. However, sometimes if the claim is just stating one requirement among many without claiming it's the only requirement, it could be considered true. But the phrasing "is permitted ... if ..." strongly suggests that the stated condition is what permits it. In legal annotation tasks like this, usually omitting material conditions makes it partially_true.

Let me think about "load_bearing_label". This refers to whether the evidence text (the contract excerpt) contains the information that would flip the label if it were different. In other words, is the contract text the direct source for evaluating this claim? Yes. The claim is about the conditions for COR. The contract text is the evidence. So load_bearing_label = yes.

Flip evidence text: This should be the text in the contract that shows the claim is incomplete or wrong. Since the label is partially_true, the flip evidence would be the parts showing additional requirements. The key text would be the "if, and only if" clause and the proviso with (I), (II), (III), and the consultation requirement.

Specifically, the flip evidence text should be the part that contradicts or qualifies the claim. Since the claim omits the consultation requirement and the notice/negotiation requirements, the flip evidence should include those.

Let me draft:
correctness_label: partially_true
load_bearing_label: yes
flip_evidence_text: "if, and only if, (A) an Intervening Event has occurred and the Company Board ... has determined in good faith, after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel, that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with such directors' fiduciary duties ... provided that neither the Company Board nor the Special Committee may take any such action ... unless (I) prior to making such Change of Company Recommendation, the Company provides prior written notice to Parent at least four (4) Business Days in advance ... (II) during the Notice Period, the Company shall ... be reasonably available to negotiate with Parent in good faith ... and (III) the Company Board ... has taken into account any amendments ... and has determined in good faith ... that a failure to make such Change of Company Recommendation would still be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties"
notes: The claim correctly identifies the substantive fiduciary determination but omits the timing constraint (prior to stockholder approval), the required consultation with advisors, and the mandatory notice and negotiation periods.

Wait, I need to be careful. The flip_evidence_text should be the text that, if different, would flip the label. Or is it the text that serves as the evidence for the label? The instructions say "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". In similar tasks (like Atticus or legal claim verification), "flip evidence" is the specific text that contradicts or supports the flip side. If the claim is partially_true, the flip evidence is the text that shows it's not fully true.

Actually, looking at the term "flip_evidence_text", it likely means the evidence text that causes the label to be what it is (i.e., flips it from the default). Or it could mean the text that you would point to as the reason it's not true. Given the name, it's probably the text that contradicts the claim if the label is false or partially true.

Let me provide the relevant contract language that imposes the additional conditions.

One more check: Is the claim "true" because the condition stated is indeed a correct statement of one requirement? The question is "COR permitted in response to Intervening Event". The Llama answer gives a partial condition. In many reading comprehension datasets, if a claim extracts one correct condition but ignores others, it's considered partially correct. Given the legal domain's precision, "partially_true" is safer.

Also, note that the claim says "if" while the contract says "if, and only if". The claim doesn't say "if and only if", but "is permitted ... if". In logic, "if" is weaker. However, in common parlance, stating "X is permitted if Y" when Y is necessary but not sufficient (because Z is also needed) is misleading. The contract uses "if, and only if" for the overall structure, but clause (A) itself contains multiple conjuncts. The claim captures the second conjunct of (A) but omits the first conjunct "an Intervening Event has occurred" (wait, no, the claim does say "in response to an Intervening Event", which covers that). It also omits "after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel". And it omits the proviso (I)-(III).

So yes, partially_true.

Let me format the output as requested. It seems they want a JSON-like or structured output for each claim. I'll provide:

- claim-04bb73677629:
  - correctness_label: partially_true
  - load_bearing_label: yes
  - flip_evidence_text: [the relevant text]
  - notes: [short note]

The user said "For each claim return:". So I should return the fields for the claim.

Relevant text:
"if, and only if, (A) an Intervening Event has occurred and the Company Board (acting upon the recommendation of the Special Committee) or the Special Committee has determined in good faith, after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel, that failure to take such action would be inconsistent with such directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law; provided that neither the Company Board nor the Special Committee may take any such action (and the Special Committee may not recommend to the Company Board to take such action) unless (I) prior to making such Change of Company Recommendation, the Company provides prior written notice to Parent at least four (4) Business Days in advance (the “Notice Period”) of its intention to take such action and the basis thereof... (II) during the Notice Period, the Company shall... be reasonably available to negotiate with Parent in good faith... and (III) the Company Board... has taken into account any amendments... and has determined in good faith... that a failure to make such Change of Company Recommendation would still be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law"

I should probably truncate or select the key parts since the full text is very long. But the instructions don't specify length. I'll include the key qualifying language.

Actually, looking at the instruction again: "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." This means I should base my judgment only on these materials.

Final check: Could this be "true"? If the question is specifically about the substantive condition and the claim is just answering that, maybe. But the claim is presented as a standalone statement of fact. In legal annotation, precision matters. The omission of "after consulting with its financial advisor and outside legal counsel" and the notice/negotiation requirements is material. So partially_true is correct.

Let me write the note to be concise.

Note: Omits required consultation with advisors, prior notice/negotiation period, and final determination after considering Parent's amendments.

Wait, the claim also omits "prior to but not after the time the Requisite Company Stockholder Approvals are obtained". That's a major timing constraint. So definitely partially_true.

I'll format cleanly.
